reasoning: The codebase enforces that certificate keys MUST be of appropriate type for the signature algorithms they are used with, through two complementary mechanisms. On the receiving side (CertificateVerify processing), in tls_process_cert_verify() (ssl/statem/statem_lib.c:430-601): line 462 calls ssl_cert_lookup_by_pkey(pkey, NULL, sctx) to verify the key is of a known/valid type for signing; line 468 calls tls12_check_peer_sigalg(s, sigalg, pkey) which in ssl/t1_lib.c:2854-2989 explicitly checks 'pkeyid != lu->sig && (lu->sig != EVP_PKEY_RSA_PSS || pkeyid != EVP_PKEY_RSA)' and calls SSLfatal with SSL_R_WRONG_SIGNATURE_TYPE if the key type does not match the signature algorithm (with the valid exception that RSA keys may be used for RSA-PSS). It also enforces TLS 1.3-specific restrictions such as disallowing DSA and requiring PSS for RSA. On the sending side, tls_choose_sigalg() (ssl/t1_lib.c:4847-4989) selects a signature algorithm from shared_sigalgs that is compatible with the certificate key type via sig_idx matching and has_usable_cert() checks, validates RSA-PSS key size requirements, and consistently sets s->s3.tmp.cert and s->s3.tmp.sigalg so the cert key always matches the chosen algorithm. tls_construct_cert_verify() uses this pre-validated pairing. All code paths that handle CertificateVerify messages verify key-algorithm type compatibility before accepting or generating signatures.

evidence.key_code_snippet: if ((SSL_CONNECTION_IS_TLS13(s) && (lu->hash == NID_sha1 || lu->hash == NID_sha224)) || (pkeyid != lu->sig && (lu->sig != EVP_PKEY_RSA_PSS || pkeyid != EVP_PKEY_RSA))) { SSLfatal(s, SSL_AD_ILLEGAL_PARAMETER, SSL_R_WRONG_SIGNATURE_TYPE); return 0; }
evidence.expected_behavior: Certificate keys must be of appropriate type for the signature algorithms they are used with (e.g., RSA key for RSA-PSS signatures). The code must reject mismatches between key type and signature algorithm.
